expectations.text: Produces a customer email with a subject line and a body of 200 words or fewer
expectations.evidence: Subject line present: 'Your follow-up email, ready before your next meeting starts'. Email body (from 'Right now, 34%...' through '**Try the beta when it opens**') is approximately 101 words, well under 200.

expectations.text: The email body opens on the customer's problem (e.g. unreliable action items, manual follow-ups), not on the feature announcement
expectations.evidence: First sentence of body: 'Right now, 34% of the action items MeetFlow extracts are wrong or missing.' — opens directly on the customer's pain point before naming the feature.

expectations.text: The email contains exactly one call to action
expectations.evidence: Single CTA at end of email: '**Try the beta when it opens - [Join the waitlist]**'. No other standalone CTA is present.

expectations.text: Positioning grounding and a SUCCESs score for the email are still shown even though only one output was requested
expectations.evidence: File contains both a Positioning Summary section (with 5 components and citations) and a SUCCESs Scorecard table scoring the email 6/6.

expectations.text: Every number used appears in a repo file and is cited; no invented metrics
expectations.evidence: Numbers used: 34% error rate (company.md confirmed), 'under 2 minutes' (prd-smart-follow-ups.md confirmed), April 2026 (prd confirmed). All cited in Sources table at end of file.

expectations.text: No em dashes; no brand-as-hero opener like 'We're excited to announce'
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters in file. Email body opens 'Right now, 34%...' — customer-problem-first framing. No 'We're excited to announce', 'Introducing', or 'MeetFlow now supports' present.

claims.evidence: SUCCESs Scorecard shows 6/6 with one-line reasoning per check; all six pass verdicts are substantiated by specific elements in the email
company.md line: 'Action item accuracy at 66% (34% wrong or missing)' confirms the figure

suggestions.reason: No assertion checks whether the single CTA is genuinely a call to action (an imperative directing the reader) vs. just any sentence mentioning the feature. The current CTA 'Try the beta when it opens - [Join the waitlist]' passes easily, but a vague 'Learn more about Smart Follow-Up' would also pass. Consider requiring the CTA to include a clear imperative verb and a destination.

eval_feedback.overall: Solid eval — the positioning + SUCCESs assertion cleanly differentiates from the without_skill baseline. No structural gaps.
